summary: Aspen's affordable-housing model is anchored in APCHA deed restrictions, the 1.0% housing Real Estate Transfer Tax (RETT), and the 277-unit Lumberyard project now under construction. The verified public record also shows a small LIHTC slice inside APCHA's portfolio, a transferable affordable-housing-credit program, regional Good Deeds funding through WMRHC, and short-term-rental permit rules tied to housing impacts.

paragraphs.text: The Aspen/Pitkin County Housing Authority (APCHA) was created in 1982 by an Intergovernmental Agreement between the City of Aspen and Pitkin County. APCHA is an independent multi-jurisdictional housing authority charged with overseeing affordable housing and has powers to acquire and dispose of property, plan, construct and manage affordable housing, contract, hire, and raise revenues.

APCHA regulations define rental and ownership units in Categories 1 through 5 and Resident Occupied (RO). In most cases, deed-restricted homes appreciate at the lesser of 3% per year or CPI, plus any appreciation allowed by the applicable deed restriction.

A CAST / NWCCOG inventory found that of Aspen's 3,278 full-time-occupied units, roughly 70% (2,303) are deed-restricted, the second-highest share among 38 Colorado mountain-resort communities surveyed plus 5 out-of-state ski towns.

sections.heading: Real Estate Transfer Tax (RETT) housing revenue base

paragraphs.text: Aspen voters approved a 1.0% RETT dedicated to addressing the local housing shortage, effective July 1, 1989. The housing RETT runs alongside a separate 0.5% RETT for the Wheeler Opera House and arts, and transfer taxes are paid by the purchasing party at closing.

On November 5, 2024, Aspen voters approved Issue 2A by 66.00% to 34.00%, extending the existing 1.0% real estate transfer tax and 0.45% sales tax that fund affordable housing and transportation initiatives.
RETT collections for calendar year 2024 totaled approximately $23.9M on 638 free-market transactions against a $19.4M budget; $15.8M of that amount was allocated to housing initiatives.

Lumberyard is an 11-acre, 277-unit, 100% affordable rental development near the Airport Business Center along Highway 82, planned as three four-story buildings serving households from below 50% AMI up to 240% AMI. The City selected Gorman & Company as developer in July 2025 and approved financial closing 'A' by Resolution #042, Series 2026, on March 24, 2026.

Construction of the first 104 units and related infrastructure began in 2026, with occupancy planned for 2028. Financial closing 'B' is planned for May 2027 for two additional buildings totaling 173 units, with occupancy planned for late 2028 and 2029. The city plans to invest $250M from its housing fund to deliver the full 277-unit buildout.

On November 4, 2025, Aspen voters approved Issue 2B by 61.92% to 38.08%, authorizing up to $70M in revenue-backed debt with a total repayment cap of $152.55M for Lumberyard. The debt is to be repaid from the existing affordable-housing RETT, with no new taxes.

APCHA's LIHTC inventory is a narrow slice of the local affordable-housing system: 127 Low-Income Housing Tax Credit units, consisting of 40 units at Aspen Country Inn and 87 units at Truscott Phase II.

In 2010 the Aspen City Council adopted an affordable-housing-credit program that gives developers a fourth mitigation option. Developers that voluntarily create deed-restricted housing receive certificates that can be sold on the free market and used to offset future housing requirements in other projects.

The West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC) was established in 2022 as a Colorado 501(c)(3) nonprofit serving the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys from Aspen to Parachute. Its membership list includes the City of Aspen, and its active programs include Good Deeds.

Aspen City Council approved a $1M contribution to WMRHC's Good Deeds program in July 2025, following a $450,000 Aspen contribution in 2024. Regional public funding in the program's inaugural year totaled $2M across participating jurisdictions.

WMRHC's Good Deeds program can provide eligible homebuyers financial assistance worth up to 30% of a home's purchase price in exchange for a permanent deed restriction that prohibits short-term rentals and second-home ownership and creates stable, price-capped workforce housing.

Aspen's short-term-rental program recognizes three permit types under Ordinance No. 9, Series 2022: Lodging Exempt permits for qualifying lodges and condo-hotels, Owner-Occupied permits limited to 120 rental nights per year, and Classic permits for non-owner-occupied or owner-occupied residential properties. Classic permits are capped in certain residential zones, while Commercial (C-1), Commercial Core (CC), Lodge (L), Commercial Lodge (CL), Lodge Overlay (LP), and Lodge Preservation Overlay (LO) have no STR-C permit limit.
